fullText:
A Paris Basement Gives a Glimpse of Future Drone Warfare by @LionelRALaurent of @opinion

"In a Paris basement office that could belong to any number of trendy software startups, entrepreneur Hadrien Canter of Alta Ares opens a crate containing a critical ingredient of modern warfare: a drone interceptor. About the size of a desk lamp, it uses artificial-intelligence software and radar to detect, catch and destroy military drones."

"France is testing several interceptors including Alta Ares models in the UAE, where it has naval and airbases that have been targeted by Iran. “With the Gulf economies looking to invest more in defense and Europe trying to lift domestic arms production, both have reason to work together,” says Giuseppe Spatafora of the European Union Institute for Security Studies think tank."

fullText:
Here's the instructions on how to get an older version of OpenClaw to use Claude Fable 5 using API key properly - paste this into your Claude Code instance operating on your openclaw.json. 

Apply the fable-5 adaptive-thinking hotfix to OpenClaw's dist.

Context: On 2026-06-10 Anthropic flipped claude-fable-5 to require adaptive-thinking params (thinking.type:"adaptive" + output_config.effort) and now rejects the legacy thinking.type:"enabled"/"disabled" style. OpenClaw hardcodes its adaptive-model list in the dist and doesn't know fable-5, so every fable-5 turn errors. The dist lives in the ephemeral container (/app/node_modules/openclaw/dist) and is wiped on rebuild.

Steps:

1. Run cd /data/.openclaw/workspace && node scripts/patch-fable5-adaptive-thinking.mjs. It's idempotent — it patches only if the marker is missing and exits 1 if anchors are gone.
2. If the script is missing, recreate it: it edits TWO dist files (anthropic-*.js and provider-stream-*.js, find by grepping for supportsAdaptiveThinking) with TWO fixes each: (a) append || modelId.includes("fable-5") || modelId.includes("fable5") to the adaptive-model classifier (the line matching modelId.includes("sonnet-4-6") || modelId.includes("sonnet-4.6");), and (b) guard the thinking-off path so params.thinking = { type: "disabled" } is only sent when !supportsAdaptiveThinking(https://t.co/cVYIBnGDOq) — omit the param entirely for adaptive models (sessions with thinking:off stay bricked otherwise; /new doesn't clear the override).
3. If it reports MISS (anchor not found), OpenClaw was updated and the dist layout changed — grep the new dist for supportsAdaptiveThinking and re-derive the anchors; or check whether the new build already classifies fable-5 as adaptive, in which case delete the script + the fable5-adaptive-thinking-patch-rearm cron job.
4. Restart the gateway to load the patched dist

fullText:
Evaluating AI Agents — Bootcamp on June 27 hosted by @PacktPublishing @PacktDataML — register with my discount code 'KIRK50' at this link to save: https://t.co/CmFrKJGHWC

Participants will be guided through the fundamentals of Evaluating AI Agents, covering:

🟠Why modern agents fail across tool usage, planning, execution trajectories, outputs, and adversarial inputs.
🟧How to build component-level evaluations to assess tool selection, argument quality, and planning effectiveness.
🔶Methods for defining and measuring trajectory-level metrics such as step count, cost, recovery behavior, and loop detection.
🟠Techniques for creating outcome-level evaluators using multidimensional rubrics and LLM-as-a-judge approaches, and calibrating them against human evaluations.
🟧Common adversarial failure modes unique to agentic systems, with a focus on indirect prompt injection through tool outputs.
🔶Frameworks for determining which evaluation layer is most relevant based on specific use cases and team maturity levels.

fullText:
Feynman Lectures on Computation (Frontiers in Physics. Anniversary Edition): https://t.co/1saYvf2cru

Amazon summary: "The last lecture course that Nobel Prize winner Richard P. Feynman gave to students at Caltech from 1983 to 1986 was not on physics but on computer science. The first edition of the Feynman Lectures on Computation, published in 1996, provided an overview of standard and not-so-standard topics in computer science given in Feynman’s inimitable style. Although now over 20 years old, most of the material is still relevant and interesting, and Feynman’s unique philosophy of learning and discovery shines through. For this new edition, Tony Hey has updated the lectures with an invited chapter from Professor John Preskill on 'Quantum Computing 40 Years Later'. This contribution captures the progress made toward building a quantum computer since Feynman’s original suggestions in 1981."

Unity Catalog and Unity AI Gateway extend governance across AI systems with identity-aware access controls, runtime policies, guardrails, auditability, observability, and cost intelligence across every agent interaction.

user.name: Kirk Borne
user.description: Advisor to startups. Freelancer. @LeadershipData founder. Global Speaker. Top #B2B influencer & social promoter #DataScience #AI #ML. PhD Astrophysics @Caltech

fullText:
🌟Enterprise Data Architecture & Foundations for Confident Decisions, Autonomous Systems at Scale, and an AI-Native Future🌟

╰┈➤Take a moment and check this out ... a new Medium publication by @PreciselyData => 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰-𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘆 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗗𝗶𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁 <= now available at https://t.co/fyBqHSeVhj

My immediate reaction was "Wow! They really get it!" — AI is nothing without a solid data foundation: data governance, quality, access, delivery, metadata, semantic context, and AI-ready data products. In short, Trusted Data! That's where @PreciselyData shines. And, I should also add, one of the Agentic-Ready Data Digest's first articles addresses the importance of third-party location data, the source for location intelligence.

In a nutshell, metadata and location intelligence provide enormously powerful context to "intelligent systems", and Agentic AI is no exception. In fact, such contextualized data is imperative for accurate, timely, and situationally relevant Agentic AI applications. This is where Enterprise AI Meets Reality, as one of the articles clearly states: https://t.co/NjEK4g6mt0 

When I was a university professor, I used to tell my data science students about the old saying, "the 3 most important things in real estate are location, location, location", and then I would follow that quote by telling them that the 3 most important things in data are metadata, metadata, metadata! Well, label it any way you want, but incorporating metadata into data-fueled applications, particularly Agentic AI, is all powerful. Meet your Enterprise AI superpower: Agentic-Ready Data, semantic metadata, location intelligence, contextual metadata, third-party data, situational data, or simply "OTHER DATA ABOUT YOUR DATA" (which, by the way, is my favorite definition for METADATA).

Note that my comments here only address some of the richness of Precisely's new Medium publication — The Agentic-Ready Data Digest.

The Agentic-Ready Enterprise requires Trusted Agentic-Ready Data. Don't set sail on the rapidly evolving seas of AI without it. Get your enterprise onboard by following and keeping up with the Agentic-Ready Data Digest at https://t.co/fyBqHSeVhj

╰┈➤Finally, be sure to visit Precisely at https://t.co/SZxwVhsWPt

user.name: François Chollet
user.description: Co-founder @ndea. Co-founder @arcprize. Creator of Keras and ARC-AGI. Author of 'Deep Learning with Python'.

fullText:
Some considerations that many folks seem not to get:

1. It can be a bubble even if the tech works. (For instance, if the tech doesn't have a high-demand use case.)

2. It can be a bubble even if the tech works and has strong product-market fit. (For instance, if the tech cannot be economically viable.)

3. It can be a bubble even if the tech works, has strong product-market fit, and has a path to eventual economic viability. (For instance, if profitability takes too long to achieve or makes margin/competition assumptions that fail to materialize.)

4. It can be a bubble even if the tech works, has strong product-market fit, and is currently highly profitable. (For instance, if demand has a hard ceiling and growth stops once the ceiling is reached.)

5. It can be a bubble even if the tech works, has strong product-market fit, is currently highly profitable, and has unlimited future demand.

Literally all it takes for something to be a bubble is for lots of people to over-enthusiastically bet their money on it, and subsequently get panicky.

Importantly, bubbles can be attached both to things that are completely hogwash, like the Metaverse, and to world-changing developments like the Internet or railways. Bubbles don't care. They're brought into existence by the thoughts and feelings of investors, not by actual tech or products.

"The bubble has burst" doesn't mean "the tech didn't work" or "people stopped using the tech." It only means that people got panicky, investor money dried up, and valuations collapsed. Internet adoption didn't stop in 2000.

user.name: Vincent Boucher · AGI.eth → ASI.eth
user.description: President, @Quebec_AI & @Montreal_AI. AGI.eth → ASI.eth. Sovereign Intelligence. Institutional Command. Govern capability before capability governs.

fullText:
AGI will not be a chatbot.

It will be an institution.

I’m sharing my paper:

AGI ALPHA: A Scalable Substrate for Intelligence Organizations

The core idea is simple:

The Transformer scaled intelligence inside models.

AGI ALPHA aims to scale intelligence across organizations.

Not more agents talking in circles.

Proof-bearing machine labor.

A job must be bounded.
A tool action must be traced.
A result must be validated.
A capability must be reusable.
A settlement must be auditable.
A claim must carry evidence.

No proof, no settlement.

No Evidence Docket, no strong claim.

That is the line between agentic AI theater and real machine labor.

Today, most AI systems optimize for impressive outputs.

AGI ALPHA asks for something harder:

verified work.

The architecture is an organizational substrate above models:

agents
jobs
validators
tools
memory
markets
settlement
governance
capacity allocation

In this framing, intelligence is not just benchmark performance.

It is governed, evidence-producing, compounding institutional work.

That distinction matters.

A model can answer.
An agent can act.
But an intelligence organization can allocate, verify, remember, settle, improve, and compound.

That is the frontier.

AGI ALPHA is claim-bounded by design.

It does not claim achieved AGI.
It does not claim ASI.
It does not claim empirical SOTA.
It does not claim civilization-scale capability.

It proposes a testable architecture and an evidence standard:

real tasks
baselines
ProofBundles
replay logs
validator reports
cost and safety ledgers
delayed outcomes
independent reproduction

The future will not be won by systems that merely appear autonomous.

It will be won by systems that can prove:

what they did
what it cost
what changed
what was learned
what risks were controlled
what capability now compounds

That is why I believe the next substrate is not only computational.

It is institutional.

Full credit:
Vincent Boucher
President, https://t.co/dCKMr5Lici and https://t.co/9VRSyr4dv4

Paper:
AGI ALPHA: A Scalable Substrate for Intelligence Organizations
https://t.co/wIFs1aAtdz

I’m attaching the first page because the thesis is worth reading directly.

The next frontier is not just bigger models.

It is institutions that make intelligence verifiable.

#AGIAlpha #QuebecAI #SovereignAI #ArtificialIntelligence #AIResearch #Agents

fullText:
You may have recently heard claims that video generation models are "dumb" about physics, and only "world models" (V-JEPA, specifically) have a valid internal model of physics.

This turns out to be false. In a recent paper, researchers show that a LINEAR probe of diffusion videogen models predict various "physics" very well, significantly better than V-JEPA or VideoMAE (and plain VAE just sucks).

This is noteworthy, because a *linear* probe being this accurate shows that the model has a pretty explicit internal representation of the physics!

fullText:
Announcing OpenSharing, a new open standard for sharing data and AI assets across platforms and organizations.

Five years ago, we pioneered open data sharing with Delta Sharing, the most widely adopted open protocol for secure zero-copy data sharing - used by thousands of partners and customers. 

Today, we're excited to announce its evolution to OpenSharing: The first open, vendor-neutral protocol for sharing AI assets, including agent skills, AI models, and unstructured data - in collaboration with partners including @OpenAI, @SAP, @LSEGplc, @stripe, @TheTradeDesk, @Minio, @AmadeusITGroup, @Atlassian, and more.

Enterprises can now use OpenSharing to share AI Assets, on-premises assets, as well as share structured and unstructured data in all formats. Hosted by The @linuxfoundation, OpenSharing creates an open foundation for data and AI collaboration in the agentic era. https://t.co/qIrOPCjr3h

fullText:
In June 2024, a group of creators began creating with Kling AI. Besides "this is amazing," we heard one phrase more than anything else: "If only."
If only characters felt more alive.
If only movement felt more real.
If only she could speak.
For two years, we heard this over and over again. And with every iteration of our product, we listened.
From Kling 1.0 to 3.0, every breakthrough began with a creator saying: "If only it could be better."
In June 2026, Kling AI has reached over 100 million users worldwide.
As we celebrate our Anniversary II, we want to say thank you to every creator.
Kling has grown with you, every step of the way.

fullText:
MEET THE WOLF PACK ROBOTS TRANSFORMING JD LOGISTICS

JD logistics has a series of robots called Langzu (wolf pack).

The idea is for swift, coordinated pack animals.

Here are 4 of them:

1: Tianlang (天狼 - Sky Wolf)

A high-speed shuttle system for multi-tier, high-density tote storage and rapid container retrieval.  

2: Dilang (地狼 - Earth Wolf)

A low-profile AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles) that lifts shelves and carries pallets across warehouse floors.

3: Zhilang (智狼 - Intelligent Wolf)

A modular Goods-to-Person (GTP) solution that combines handling AGVs and lifting robots

These can leverage vertical spaces up to 10 meters high, tripling traditional picking speeds.  

4: Yilang (意朗 - Intuitive Wolf)

Robotic arms equipped with force sensors and machine vision.

These are optimized for sorting irregular parcels or folding boxes at high speed.

user.name: Natasha Jaques
user.description: Assistant Professor leading the Social RL Lab https://t.co/ykwfJG84Bj @uwcse and Staff Research Scientist at @GoogleAI.

fullText:
Really enjoyed reading the Microsoft MAI-Thinking-1 "Building a Hill Climbing Machine" paper. Amazing they publicly released all the info needed to train a frontier model, down to hparams.

I also thought this was pretty telling:
- pre-training: 30 trillion tokens
- mid-training (SFT on STEM/math/code data): 3.55 trillion tokens
- RL post-training: 150 billion tokens.
Looks like @ylecun was right all along with the cake analogy.

Obviously I still think something like RL (optimizing for long term goals) is fundamental to what we think of as intelligence. But it's not the volume of learning signal, it's the optimization on top of an already reasonable predictive model.

fullText:
XGBoost for Regression, Predictive Modeling, and Time Series Analysis — Learn how to build, evaluate, & deploy predictive models:
https://t.co/DsGwnEXyWF v/ @PacktDataML
—
My review:

🟠XGBoost is definitely the focal point and central contribution of this book, along with all those other things: Regression Trees, Predictive Modeling, Time Series Analysis, and Forecasting. 

🟠This book’s added value comes in its thorough progression from start to finish with comprehensive coverage of all the major steps and issues in data science and machine learning modeling: data cleaning, imbalanced data, feature engineering, feature engineering pipeline, feature importance, categorical data encoding, model interpretability and explainability, ensembles, model training, model evaluation metrics, hyperparameter tuning, model deployment, and more - including a quick-start case study starter project that feeds into next-level projects. 

🟠I highly recommend this broadly useful book for all levels of practitioners.

user.description: Databricks is the Data and AI company, helping organizations build and scale data and AI apps, analytics and agents.

fullText:
💳 Card fraud happens in seconds, but many detection systems still rely on slow batch processing or complex streaming architectures.

This open-source Solution Accelerator shows how teams can build an end-to-end fraud detection workflow with Spark Real-Time Mode and Lakebase, including low-latency ML scoring, explainable fraud signals, and live monitoring on a unified platform.

With it, organizations can achieve sub-second fraud intervention while reducing operational complexity and avoiding the need for separate streaming infrastructure. 

https://t.co/fNdmwMgVs3
